content.problemStatement:
You are given a permutation PPP of length NNN.

For each prefix length 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N:


Consider the prefix subarray A=[P1,P2,…,Pi]A = [P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_i]A=[P1​,P2​,…,Pi​].
Perform the following operation on AAA any (possibly zero) number of times:

Select three **distinct** indices j,k,j, k,j,k, and lll such that:

1≤j<k<l≤∣A∣1 \leq j < k < l \leq |A|1≤j<k<l≤∣A∣
Aj>Ak<AlA_j > A_k < A_lAj​>Ak​<Al​


Delete AkA_kAk​ from AAA, and concatenate the remaining elements without changing the order.
In case you want to perform more operations, you will perform them on the updated AAA.


Find the number of **distinct** arrays AAA that can be generated through the operations. Since the answer might be huge, please print it modulo 998244353998244353998244353.
Note that a permutation of length NNN consists of all integers from 111 to NNN in any order.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the permutation PPP.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1,P_2,\ldots,P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​ representing the permutation PPP.




Output Format
For each test case, print NNN space-separated integers, denoting the answer modulo 998244353998244353998244353 for each prefix length.
constraints: All elements of PPP are distinct.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1055 \cdot 10^55⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111:

A=[4]A = [4]A=[4]: You cannot perform any operation so this is the only array possible.
A=[4,1]A = [4, 1]A=[4,1]: You cannot perform any operation so this is the only array possible.
A=[4,1,3]A = [4, 1, 3]A=[4,1,3]: Select indices j=1,k=2,j=1,k=2,j=1,k=2, and l=3l=3l=3. We can remove Ak=1A_k = 1Ak​=1 and the array becomes A=[4,3]A = [4,3]A=[4,3]. No further operations can be performed.
Thus, two possible arrays for this prefix are [4,1,3][4,1,3][4,1,3] and [4,3][4,3][4,3].
A=[4,1,3,2]A = [4, 1, 3, 2]A=[4,1,3,2]: Select indices j=1,k=2,j=1,k=2,j=1,k=2, and l=3l=3l=3. We can remove Ak=1A_k = 1Ak​=1 and the array becomes A=[4,3,2]A = [4,3,2]A=[4,3,2]. No further operations can be performed.
Thus, two possible arrays for this prefix are [4,1,3,2][4,1,3,2][4,1,3,2] and [4,3,2][4,3,2][4,3,2].
